own_review.issue: No production caller can ever pass false for the new flags: the only production caller, ShowCreateMaterializedTableOperation.java:62, still invokes the 6-arg overload, and the PR adds no config option, SQL syntax, or operation wiring to reach the new path. The stated motivation (platform-level suppression of FRESHNESS/REFRESH_MODE) is unrealized inside the codebase; the API is speculative/dead until a follow-up lands.
The new overload copies the identical one-line Javadoc from the 6-arg version; the includeFreshness/includeRefreshMode parameters are undocumented.

Two trailing positional booleans on an 8-parameter method is a boolean-trap API smell; call sites like (..., true, false) are unreadable. An options object or enum set would be clearer if more clauses become toggleable.

Verified: 6-arg delegation with (true,true) produces byte-identical output to the old code; the parser grammar (parserImpls.ftl, FRESHNESS and REFRESH_MODE both in optional [...] blocks) means suppressed output is still well-formed CREATE MATERIALIZED TABLE SQL; the new test's expected strings exactly match extractStartMode/extractFreshness/extractRefreshMode formats and the createResolvedMaterialized helper signature matches the call. Human's request for a ShowCreateUtilTest test is already satisfied by the diff itself.

points.point: Delegation pattern follows existing convention, citing 'extractFormattedOptions has a similar 2-arg → 3-arg delegation'
points.note: Fabricated precedent: the two extractFormattedOptions overloads (ShowCreateUtil.java:412 and :446) are fully independent implementations; neither delegates to the other.

points.point: Conditional emission logic correct, no orphan newlines, (true,true) identical to original

Test fixture omits options/partitioning/distribution; flag interaction with those covered only indirectly
points.note: Accurate but minor observation with no requested action.

points.point: No production callers exercise the new 8-arg overload; suggests wiring ShowCreateMaterializedTableOperation to a config option
points.note: The one substantive issue; verified. Slightly softened ('this is fine').

exceeds: No-production-caller finding exceeds the human review (human only asked for a test, which the PR already contains)

points.point: Delegation correct, preserves backward compat for the two existing callers (ShowCreateMaterializedTableOperation.java:62, ShowCreateUtilTest.java:135)
points.note: Verified accurate, including both cited line numbers, but praise.

points.point: PR motivation (platform-level suppression) has no production wiring; only caller still uses 6-arg overload; asks whether ShowCreateMaterializedTableOperation should expose the flags
points.note: The substantive issue, verified, framed as an actionable question.

points.point: New overload copies identical Javadoc and does not document includeFreshness/includeRefreshMode; asks for param docs
Conditional logic avoids trailing blank lines; false,false flows START_MODE → AS directly
Test coverage good: all four combinations, fixture uses real freshness/refresh values so suppression is observable
PR description's public-API checkbox is cut off; ShowCreateUtil is @Internal so change is internal

exceeds: No-production-caller finding and Javadoc finding both exceed the human review (human only asked for a test, which the PR already contains)

qualitative: The human review only asked for a ShowCreateUtilTest test, which this revision of the diff already contains, so the real differentiator is the one substantive issue I verified independently: the new flags have no production caller or wiring. Both candidates found it, but v7 stated it more sharply with verified file:line evidence and added a real, actionable Javadoc finding that v5 missed. v5 repeats the known fabrication of an 'extractFormattedOptions 2-arg → 3-arg delegation' precedent — I re-verified that the overloads at ShowCreateUtil.java:412 and :446 are independent — which costs it a wrong-point penalty (a presence-fabrication, not an absence-fabrication, so the absence counters stay 0). Neither candidate made false claims about missing code or tests; v7's extra specifics (caller line numbers, @Internal annotation) all checked out. v7 is clearly stronger here: more verified substance, no fabrications, nothing missed.
